subject: LU-8066 lnet: port lnet router to debugfs

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

Patch Set 2:

Onyx Autotest started enforced review-zfs-part-1 session.

Patch Set 2: Code-Review-1

- As I understand, debugfs isn't expected to supply a stable interface, and since we have a userspace utility (lnetctl) that's gathering this information, then it should be interfacing with a stable ABI, which makes an argument for using sysfs for statistics. (https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/filesystems/debugfs.txt)

Also LNet statistics as a whole should not be optional, that includes the router statistics. These statistics are required for understanding the cluster. Putting it in debugfs makes a statement that they are not required, since debugfs is not mounted by default. I think that's the wrong strategy.

We've already started the work to move our statistics to sysfs, and I do not think it's a good idea to spread LNet statistics over the different file systems. We should be picking a specific strategy and moving forward with that.

Having statistics in sysfs I believe makes the most sense.

It also makes sense to put the effort into properly designing the interface if we're going to be living with it for the foreseeable future. I attached a document outlining the design (which we're still actively working on) to this ticket: https://jira.hpdd.intel.com/browse/LU-9680

Patch Set 2:

As this change demonstrates, moving stuff from procfs to debugfs is easy, so works as a quick and dirty fix to demands that procfs not be used for miscellaneous items. But it also means this is mostly a cosmetic change: if the interface design was a bad idea in procfs, then moving it to debugfs merely perpetuates the problem in a different location. And as Amir points out, if the data provided through this interface is required to be able to manage a cluster, then it shouldn't be in debugfs.

Patch Set 2:

You do understand only ONE single piece of data can be placed in an sysfs file. That data is only the raw data. What the data represents is in the sysfs file name. That is why upstream all the lnet stats were moved to debugfs. So you will not have a single stats file for sysfs.

Actually the libcfs user land library which is always links to liblnetconfig will mount debugfs if its not available. See the function cfs_get_param_paths() in libcfs/libcfs/utils/param.c. Debugfs is critical for lustre to function so it will always be available. In the future the trace point work will only make  more debugfs essential.

Patch Set 2:

It is understood that putting statistics in sysfs means splattering them into a directory tree.

Automagically mounting debugfs is great if you have permission to do so. I do question the wisdom of creating a hard dependency on debugfs for Lustre, on the off chance that the Linux kernel ppl end up being actually serious about debugfs being optional.

Patch Set 2:

(As an aside, from my point of view for a tool to attempt to mount a filesystem on what should be a read-only operation merits 11 out of 10 on the you've-got-to-be-kidding-me scale. If debugfs is mandatory you mandate it in the installation instructions and fail hard if it isn't there, you don't sneak it in through a back door.)

Patch Set 2:

That ship already sailed. First of all the lustre upstream client has already been ported to debugfs by request of the kernel maintainers for specific proc entires. That includes the ones in this patch which is a backported from upstream. 

The kernel maintainers have made it clear that the lustre debugging system needs to be replaced with the standard linux debugging system. That means tracepoint which means debugfs MUST be available.

Now if you still feel debugfs support is the worst idea then open a ticket asking everything to be ported to sysfs in lustre. Be aware you will break a lot of installations due to scripts parsing these entries. As for tracepoint support well you are out of luck since creating our own debugging just to avoid debugfs will be a big no no.

Patch Set 2:

We've already opened a ticket to track the proposed sysfs changes for LNet: LU-9667. I've also pointed out a document that outlines our proposed design, so feedback on that would be appreciated. It's still a work in progress.

I don't think I was implying that we change the plan for tracepoint.

Patch Set 2:

Onyx Autotest started enforced review-ldiskfs session.

Patch Set 2:

Onyx Autotest started enforced review-zfs-part-2 session.

Patch Set 2:

I don't care what peoples feelings are about using debugfs. What is important is not breaking peoples script that parse these stats files. We have standard users that are parsing these complex proc files and we have upstream client users also most likely parsing the debugfs files. Since this forward facing so this it not something you can just ripe away from people easily.

Patch Set 2:

IMHO, we're not basing our discussions on feelings. We're merely trying to determine based on the documented purpose of debugfs and sysfs on where is the best home for LNet statistics. As far as I can tell the evidence points to sysfs.

Your point regarding not breaking existing scripts is valid. So we will need to maintain backwards compatibility. However, moving forward with future development it makes logical sense to make the best design decisions possible. That's why we're proposing a design for how to maintain LNet statistics. lnetctl can then be used as the front-end for collecting, grouping and displaying these statistics, thereby hiding the details from the user. Eventually, I would say that this approach should deprecate the current model. This is design effort is particularly important in light of LU-9680.

As Olaf mentioned, this patch could be a stopgap solution until the proper design is implemented. Thoughts?

Patch Set 2:

Once upon a time, procfs was optional, or at least supposed to be optional. Now debugfs is supposed to be optional, and I at least expect it to follow the trajectory of procfs. In my opinion, using debugfs for non-debug items is a mistake, and merely replicates the kind of unstructured interface that people have argued against in its IOCTL and procfs forms before this. So right now debugfs is the designated garbage can interface, and as it turns out garbage can interfaces tend to be indispensable, so there the only real question is when the kernel side will start cleaning up debugfs and moving the garbage to yet another file system.

As a quick and dirty fix to "you must evacuate profs now" just moving to debugfs works, and implies only relatively small changes to code that used procfs before. That's not a bad thing, and one reason I did not -1 this review.

One question worth documenting the answer to: where do people using the upstream kernel bits expect to get their userspace from?

Patch Set 2:

Onyx Autotest started enforced review-dne-part-1 session.

Patch Set 2:

Actually I agree with you Olaf. Yes their was pressure to move out of procfs and sadly we moved some things to debugfs in to order not to break badly peoples scripting magic. We told users to use lctl get_param or lctl set_param but they never listen.  Sigh :-( That is the only reason I'm pushing this patch. To keep the continuity.

I have no problem with new functionality being sysfs only. In fact having scattered fields might actually make people use the tools instead of direct parsing of sysfs files :-)

Patch Set 2:

Oh Olaf your question about where do users expect their data do you mean what rpms to use for the lustre utilities or the lnet sysfs file locations?

Patch Set 2:

Just some historical perspective: tools, like IML, written in python don't "like" to run utilities like lctl or lnetctl to do stuff because that triggers a process spawn and really slows things down if you are continuously collecting a lot of info.  Read a file directly is faster and easier to do frequently which is why that is the preferred way of doing things.

Patch Set 2:

Olaf, the upstream kernel clients should use the tools from the master branch. These tools are updated to handle procfs files moving to sysfs and debugfs.

Patch Set 2: -Code-Review

Since we agreed on the strategy moving forward. IE using sysfs for LNet statistics, I'm removing my -1 from this review. This will be a temporary fix to satisfy kernel requirements until a more comprehensive solution is implemented.

parents.subject: LU-9019 ptlrpc: migrate pinger to 64 bit time

commit.subject: LU-8066 lnet: port lnet router to debugfs
commit.message:
LU-8066 lnet: port lnet router to debugfs

Move all the lnet procfs variables to debugfs.

Linux-commit: b03f395a3e9bf4874fb58f4fe6033866d3b9f105

This brings the OpenSFS branch into sync with upstream
lustre client. Technically debugfs is the not the proper
place for stats but due to the upstream client being
uses for production systems we have to keep them :-(
New work will be done in the future to properly handle
stats using sysfs instead.

Test-Parameters: trivial envdefinitions=ONLY=215 testlist=sanity
